trajectory_summary: The call opens with a cooperative, self-directing customer who has already done the work of identifying his need (battery backup) and his constraint (existing 10kW system). The rep navigates the orphaned-flow disclosure adequately at the surface but never investigates the prior Ion quote, misses the reframe opportunity when Ken calls the call 'duplicative,' and delivers a DQ via a specialist ruling that is not grounded in any documented Ion policy. The call ends with no appointment, no alternative path, and no warmth recovered — a flat exit on a lead that arrived warm.
outcome_dispute: The v1 outcome tag is unknown, but the DQ rationale delivered at 05:31 — 'your kilowatt size is too big for a battery or a backup' — is not supported by any documented Ion policy in the schema. The schema confirms Ion carries the Tesla Powerwall with no documented kilowatt ceiling that would exclude a standard residential system. A 10kW solar array is a common residential installation size. The specialist's ruling may be incorrect, and if so, this is a wrongful DQ on a warm, qualified, battery-interested homeowner. Manager should verify with the specialist team whether this ruling was accurate before treating the outcome as a legitimate DQ.

primary_coaching_focus.topic: Prior-contact investigation — the miss that created the 'duplicative' objection
primary_coaching_focus.quote: I think I got a quote kinda over the phone. But Okay.
primary_coaching_focus.why: Ken disclosed a prior Ion quote at 02:03 and the rep responded with 'Okay' and moved on. That single miss cascaded into the 'duplicative' objection at 03:10, which the rep then had no reframe for. The coaching priority is not the 03:10 moment — it's the 02:03 moment that made 03:10 inevitable. One question ('What did you think of it?') changes the entire second half of this call.

key_moments.quote: This is Bucky with ION reaching out about the solar form you guys filled out online. How you doing?
key_moments.stewart_read: The rep uses the standard form-fill intro on what is almost certainly a recycled lead — Ken immediately discloses an existing system and a prior Ion quote, suggesting this is not a first-touch organic lead. The intro is technically compliant but contextually mismatched. No harm done at this moment, but it signals the rep did not review lead history before dialing.

key_moments.quote: I think I got a quote kinda over the phone. But Okay.
key_moments.stewart_read: Ken's first disclosure of a prior Ion quote is the moment the rep needed to investigate: who quoted it, what was the number, what did Ken think of it. Instead the rep responds with 'Okay' and pivots immediately to contract-status. This is the root cause of the 'duplicative' objection at 03:10 — the rep had the information and didn't use it. Strategic read rejected: there is no later move the rep sets up here; the pivot to contract-status is a checklist move, not a setup.

key_moments.quote: I already talked to somebody about and they gave me a quote. So I I don't know. This is kinda duplicative.
key_moments.stewart_read: Ken's 'duplicative' objection is the second surface of the prior-quote concern — and the rep's second miss. The schema rebuttal for 'already saw a design' calls for comparison framing: 'it's always best to get a couple looks so you have something to compare against.' The rep instead offers a hold-and-transfer with no reframe. The objection was predictable from 02:03 and the rep had the tool to handle it.

key_moments.quote: It was on the high side, I thought.
key_moments.stewart_read: Ken's price objection on the prior quote is a flip opportunity the rep walks past entirely. The rep responds with a hold offer rather than inverting the objection. The inversion available here: Ken's existing system is 15+ years old, his power goes out frequently, and he's already paying for a system that doesn't give him backup — the prior quote being 'high' is the cost of not solving the problem. The rep had the emotional material to make this move and didn't.
